trajectory_summary: The call opened cold, briefly warmed when Bobby showed prior knowledge of solar credits and asked a direct cost question, then collapsed when Bobby disclosed a job emergency and Carter released him without locking a callback time. The outcome is an unanchored callback — Bobby has Carter's personal contact info and a vague promise to email, but made no time commitment. This is a low-probability follow-up, not a booked lead.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the call should be classified as 'unanchored_callback' rather than any form of booked or tentative appointment. Bobby explicitly deferred ('I'll email you in a week'), Carter accepted without locking a time or gathering any verify data, and the follow-up obligation sits entirely with Carter. No mutual commitment was made. The two enthusiasm signals Bobby showed (credits awareness at 01:00, tax credit interest at 03:38) and his volunteered home size (2,000 sq ft) are the only recoverable assets from this call.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion on cost gate — converting deflection into curiosity
primary_coaching_focus.quote: you know, I was just interested. I was trying to find out before I go any further. How much is it gonna cost me in in the end?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bobby stated his gating condition twice in under a minute. Carter deflected both times without creating any emotional hook. The schema flip was available both times and unused. Until Carter can convert a cost question into a bill-pain anchor, he will lose every Bobby-type lead at the same moment. This is the single highest-leverage skill gap on this call.

key_moments.quote: you know, I was just interested. I was trying to find out before I go any further. How much is it gonna cost me in in the end?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bobby stated his gating condition in plain language: cost is the unlock. Carter deflected correctly (no fabricated price) but missed the flip entirely. The schema move here is to redirect Bobby's cost anxiety back at his utility bill — 'what you're already paying is the cost we're trying to replace.' Carter instead described the appointment format, which gave Bobby no emotional reason to commit. This is the earliest leverage point in a multi-failure call.

key_moments.quote: I know you can sell the credits.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bobby volunteered prior knowledge of solar credits unprompted — this is not a blank-slate skeptic. He has done some research. Carter acknowledged with 'Yeah' and moved to a financial claim rather than expanding on Bobby's existing interest. A top setter uses this as the entry point into verify.interest_in_solar: 'What got you looking into it?' would have opened the emotional anchor Carter never found.

key_moments.quote: No. It's not. I'm I'm on a call. I'm a plumber, and I got a emergency call for a house in Inwood, to get the water back on. She's having a party tonight.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bobby disclosed a genuine job emergency — mid-call, mid-job, someone's water is off. Carter's response was 'Okay. So let me actually do this' and moved immediately to logistics. Yielding the call was correct; Carter should not push a customer mid-emergency. The failure was what happened next: Carter accepted Bobby's unstructured 'email me in a week' without locking any callback time. The empathy miss is not that Carter yielded — it's that he didn't acknowledge the situation humanly before releasing, and he left with no mutual commitment.

key_moments.quote: I appreciate it. Of course. And what I did, I shot you a text. So you have my personal number and personal bank number, and then I'll send you my email over text as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter closed by handing Bobby his personal contact info and promising to send his email — no locked time, no mutual commitment, no verify data collected. The follow-up obligation is entirely Carter's. This is a soft outcome with no structural anchor: Bobby said 'I'll email you in a week' and Carter accepted it. The 'personal bank number' phrase in the transcript is also anomalous and should be flagged for audio verification (see coaching implication).
